title: Write a Dockerfile
description: Produce a secure, small, well-layered Dockerfile.
content:
Write a production Dockerfile for a {{stack}} app.

Requirements:
- Multi-stage build to keep the final image small.
- Run as a non-root user.
- Pin base image versions; no `latest`.
- Order layers so dependency installs cache well.
- Include a healthcheck and sensible default command.
- No secrets baked into the image.

Explain the key choices in a few comments.

title: Author a CI Pipeline
description: Design a CI workflow that catches problems fast and cheaply.
content:
Write a CI pipeline for {{platform}} for a {{stack}} project.

Stages: install (cached), lint, typecheck, test, build. Make it fail fast, run independent steps in parallel, and cache dependencies between runs. Add the gate that should block merge. Keep it readable and explain any non-obvious step. Note what I'd add later for deployment but leave out for now.

variables.name: platform
variables.description: CI system (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, etc.)

title: Debug a Failing Deploy
description: Systematically diagnose a deployment that won't come up.
content:
My deployment is failing: {{symptom}}

Walk me through diagnosis in order of likelihood:
1. What to check first (logs, events, health, config, resources).
2. How to tell apart a config error, a resource limit, a dependency outage, and a code bug.
3. The exact commands/queries to run at each step.

Guide me iteratively based on what I report. Prioritize getting it back up (rollback?) over root cause if this is production.

title: Write Infrastructure as Code
content:
Write {{tool}} configuration to provision: {{infrastructure}}

Requirements:
- Parameterize environment-specific values; no hardcoded secrets or account IDs.
- Apply least-privilege access.
- Tag resources for ownership and cost tracking.
- Make it idempotent and safe to re-apply.

Explain what a `plan`/dry-run would show and what to double-check before applying to production.

title: Set Up Observability
description: Instrument a service with metrics, logs, and traces that matter.
content:
Help me make this service observable: {{service}}

Define: the handful of metrics that actually indicate health (think RED/USE), what to log at each level, where tracing spans add value, and the 3-5 alerts worth paging on (symptom-based, not cause-based). Avoid alert noise and vanity metrics. Tell me what 'good' looks like for each signal.

title: Write a Deployment Runbook
description: Document how to deploy and roll back a service safely.
content:
Write a deployment runbook for: {{service}}

Include: pre-deploy checks, the exact deploy steps, how to verify success (and the specific signals to watch), the rollback procedure, and who to contact if it goes wrong. Write it so a tired on-call engineer can follow it at 3am without prior context. Note the one step most likely to be skipped under pressure.

title: Incident Response Walkthrough
description: Run a calm, structured response to a live incident.
content:
I have a live incident: {{incident}}

Help me respond methodically:
1. Stabilize first — what's the fastest safe mitigation (rollback, scale, feature-flag off)?
2. What to communicate, to whom, and how often.
3. What signals to watch to confirm we're recovering.
4. What to capture now for the postmortem.

Prioritize stopping customer pain over understanding root cause. Tell me the immediate next action.

title: Harden a Shell Script
description: Make a shell script safe, portable, and predictable.
content:
Review and harden this shell script:

```
{{script}}
```

Apply: `set -euo pipefail`, quote all expansions, validate inputs, handle spaces/edge cases in paths, and fail loudly on error. Flag any non-portable or dangerous construct (unguarded `rm -rf`, unvalidated `eval`, parsing `ls`). Show the hardened version with brief comments on each change.
